notes.anchor: by the death of our cousin Lucius
notes.note: L. Tullius Cicero, the orator's first cousin and close companion, who had accompanied him to Greece and on his Sicilian quaestorship; his recent death is the occasion of this letter's opening lament.

A legal or financial matter at Rome involving one Acutilius that Atticus had entrusted to Cicero before leaving for Greece; Cicero, finding no dispute necessary, passed the advisory role to the jurist Peducaeus.

Usucapio: acquisition of full ownership by uninterrupted possession for a fixed term (one year for movables, two for land) under Roman law. Cicero's point in the next sentence is that property held in statutory guardianship was exempt from usucaption.

notes.anchor: in the case of statutory guardianship
notes.note: Tutela legitima: guardianship over a woman or minor assigned by law (typically to the nearest male agnate) rather than by a will. Cicero corrects Atticus: a ward's property under such guardianship could not be lost by usucaption.
Atticus had bought an estate in Epirus (north-west Greece), near Buthrotum, where he would build his celebrated retreat; Cicero approves the acquisition.
